description: Every statement is either true or false — there is no third option.

hints.text: This is the **law of excluded middle**: $A \lor \lnot A$. It's classical — you'll need `not-intro` and `dne`. The strategy: assume $\lnot (A \lor \lnot A)$, derive a [contradiction|contradiction], then use [double-negation|double negation]. Start with that [assumption|assumption].

From this [assumption|assumption], you'll derive $A \lor \lnot A$ itself — a [contradiction|contradiction]. To get $\lnot A$ (which gives you the right half of the [disjunction|disjunction]), assume $A$ and show it's impossible. Start: assume $A$.
From $A$, form $A \lor \lnot A$ with `or-intro-l` — placing $A$ on the left and $\lnot A$ on the right.
Now you need a step that has $\lnot (A \lor \lnot A)$ in the same [context|context] as $A$. Merge $A$ (step 2) with $\lnot (A \lor \lnot A)$ (step 1) using `and-intro`.
Extract $\lnot (A \lor \lnot A)$ from the [conjunction|conjunction]. Its [context|context] now contains $A$.

Step 3 proves $A \lor \lnot A$ with $A$ in [context|context]. Step 5 proves $\lnot (A \lor \lnot A)$ with $A$ in context. Apply `not-intro A` — this [assumption|discharges] $A$ and gives you $\lnot A$.
With $\lnot A$ in hand, build $A \lor \lnot A$ by putting $\lnot A$ on the right side using `or-intro-r`.

Now the payoff. Step 1 proves $\lnot (A \lor \lnot A)$. Step 7 proves $A \lor \lnot A$. Both share $\lnot (A \lor \lnot A)$ in [context|context]. Apply `not-intro` to get $\lnot \lnot (A \lor \lnot A)$.
One `dne` strips the [double-negation|double negation], and the law of excluded middle stands proved.
